title: Working with the part that believes it is broken beyond repair
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The part that whispers you're too far gone

I know this part. I've sat with it for years. It's the one that wakes up at 3am and tells you, quietly, that everyone else got better but you didn't. It's the part that looks at your healing journey and sees a pile of broken pieces that no amount of therapy, meditation, or self-help can ever reassemble. Right?

Here's the thing. This part isn't wrong about what it feels. The feeling of being broken beyond repair is real. It has weight. It has a history. It has reasons for existing that deserve your attention, not your shame about having it in the first place.

I used to fight this part constantly. I'd wake up with that familiar heaviness in my chest, that voice saying "you're too damaged," and I'd immediately try to argue with it. I'd pull out affirmations. I'd try to positive-think my way out of it. I'd tell myself that I just needed to try harder, meditate longer, heal faster. And every time I did that, the part got louder. It dug in deeper. It proved its point: see, you can't even handle having this thought without falling apart.

That's the paradox. The part that believes you're broken beyond repair isn't your enemy. It's actually trying to protect you from something. Something that happened a long time ago. Something that made perfect sense for it to believe.

Where this part comes from

Let me tell you what I've learned from studying people like Bessel van der Kolk and his work on how trauma lives in the body. When you experience overwhelming events especially in childhood, your nervous system does something . It creates a part of you that holds the memory, the feeling, the belief that you didn't survive. Or that you shouldn't have survived. Or that something fundamental about you is wrong.

This isn't just a thought. It's a survival adaptation. Think about it. If you grew up in a home where your needs weren't met, where you were ignored or criticized or hurt, your developing brain had to make sense of that. And for a child, the only explanation that keeps the attachment system intact is this: there must be something wrong with me. Because if there's something wrong with me, then maybe I can fix it. Maybe I can be good enough. Maybe then I'll be safe.

That's the logic of a traumatized child's mind. It's not rational. It's not fair. But it's deeply, profoundly protective. And that child part doesn't just go away when you turn 18 or 30 or 50. It's still there, carrying that belief, waiting for you to finally prove it wrong or, more likely, prove it right.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker does an incredible job of naming these parts and showing how they operate in adult life. Reading that book felt like someone finally handed me a map of the territory I'd been wandering in for decades.

What happens when we try to fix this part

Here's where most healing approaches go wrong. They tell you to replace the belief. They tell you to think positive thoughts, to affirm your worth, to just let go of the past. And that sounds good in theory. But it doesn't work. Not really. Not for this part.

Why? Because this part isn't stupid. It knows what it experienced. It knows that you were neglected, abandoned, hurt, betrayed. It knows that people who were supposed to love you didn't show up. It knows that the world proved itself unsafe. And when you try to slap a positive affirmation on top of that knowledge, the part just rolls its eyes. It's like putting a bandaid on a broken leg.

I spent years trying to convince this part that I was okay. That I was worthy. That I was lovable. And every time I did, I felt more like a fraud. Because underneath the affirmation, the part was still there, whispering "but you're not." And it was right. Not about being broken. But about the fact that the pain was real and hadn't been acknowledged.

Janina Fisher, who writes extensively about trauma and the self, talks about this exact dynamic. She says that parts don't need to be argued with or fixed. They need to be listened to. They need to be understood. They need someone to finally say "I see you. I hear you. And I understand why you believe what you believe."

That's a very different approach from trying to change the belief. It's an approach that starts with validation, not correction. And that's where real movement becomes possible.

How to work with this part without fighting it

So how do we actually do this? How do we work with the part that believes it's broken beyond repair without getting swallowed by it or fighting it?

First, you have to learn to notice it without becoming it. That's harder than it sounds. When this part activates, it doesn't feel like a part of you. It feels like all of you. It feels like objective truth. The thought "I am broken" doesn't feel like a belief. It feels like a fact about the universe.

But here's the thing. If you can learn to notice that thought as a thought, as a feeling in your body, as a familiar contraction in your chest or a heaviness in your shoulders, you've already created a tiny bit of space. And that space is everything.

I started by just saying to myself, when that part showed up, "Oh, there you are. I know you." Not with resistance. Not with frustration. Just with recognition. Like greeting an old acquaintance who shows up at your door at inconvenient times. "Hello again. I see you're here."

Then I started asking it questions. Not interrogating it. Just curious. "What do you need me to know right now?" "How old do you feel when this belief gets loud?" "What would happen if I stopped trying to fix you?"

The answers surprised me. This part wasn't trying to ruin my life. It was trying to protect me from hope. Because hope had been dangerous. Every time I'd hoped as a child, I'd been disappointed. Every time I'd believed things could be different, they got worse. So this part learned that the safest position was to assume the worst. To believe I was broken. Because then I couldn't be let down again.

That's the protection. It's not a broken belief. It's a broken trust in the possibility of healing. And that trust can't be restored by force. It can only be restored by showing up, over and over, with patience and presence.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz. He created Internal Family Systems, which is the framework I'm drawing from here. The title says it all. There are no bad parts. Even the ones that seem to be working against you are trying to help in some way.

The slow work of earning trust

I'm not going to tell you that this part will just go away once you understand it. That's not how it works. This part has been carrying a heavy load for a very long time. It's not going to hand over the keys just because you had one good conversation with it.

What happens instead is gradual. You notice the part. You acknowledge it. You thank it for its protection. You ask what it needs. And then you do that again. And again. And again. Over months. Over years.

Sometimes the part will let you see a memory it's been holding. Sometimes it will let you feel a grief that's been buried for decades. Sometimes it will just sit quietly next to you, not believing you yet, but willing to stay in the room with you.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link) by Judith Herman. She writes about how trauma destroys the sense of connection to others and to the self. And how recovery happens in stages. First safety. Then remembrance and mourning. Then reconnection. The part that believes it's broken is stuck in the first stage. It doesn't feel safe enough to let go of its belief.

So your job isn't to convince it. Your job is to create enough safety that it can eventually, on its own timeline, consider the possibility that things might be different.

For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Running on Empty (paid link) by Jonice Webb. She talks about how growing up with unmet emotional needs creates a deep sense of something being wrong with you. That's the same part. The one that believes it's broken because no one showed up to show it otherwise.

Notice what just happened in your body. Did something shift when you read that? Did that part get a little quieter? Or a little louder? Either way is fine. Just notice.

The truth is, I don't know if this part ever fully goes away. Mine hasn't. It still shows up. Especially when I'm tired, when I'm triggered, when life feels hard. But here's what's changed. I don't believe it anymore. Not completely. I can hear it, acknowledge it, even appreciate it, without letting it run the show.

And that, I think, is the real healing. Not the absence of the part. But the ability to be with it without being consumed by it. To hold it in awareness while also knowing that you are more than this belief. You are the one who can hold this part. You are the container for this pain. And that container can expand, slowly, to include more and more of what this part has been carrying.

Sit with that. Let it land. You're not broken. You're just carrying a part that learned to believe you were. And that part deserves your compassion, not your rejection.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link) by Judith Herman
  • Running on Empty (paid link) by Jonice Webb
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) by Pete Walker
